Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd mostly just listen to lecture recordings whenever I felt like it, usually late at night or while doing other things around my apartment. I know I should have been more structured, but I've always learned better by hearing things than by sitting down with books. My 20 hours a week sounds like a lot, but it was scattered and pretty casual.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go weeks barely touching anything, then cram when something was due. Deadlines were really the only thing that got me to focus, otherwise I'd just drift along assuming I'd figure it out later. It wasn't ideal but somehow it worked out for my exam scores.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or boredom. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if anything new was posted, but I wouldn't always do much once I was there. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in a few extra times because earning those badges felt kind of satisfying, even if it was silly.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got into a proper routine with the LMS. The closest thing to a habit was checking the discussion board occasionally because some of my classmates posted interesting stuff. But calling it part of my normal routine would be a stretch—it was more like sporadic check-ins when I remembered.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them while doing other things, rewind parts I didn't catch, and it just clicked better than reading. The discussion posts were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives sometimes clarified things the professor said.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and any PDF resources. I'd download them intending to read later but rarely did. Reading just doesn't stick for me the way listening does. I also skipped a lot of the optional quizzes because they felt like busywork when I already understood the material from the lectures.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would directly help me understand something or if it was actually engaging. The gamified tasks were surprisingly effective at making me feel like I was accomplishing something. But if something felt repetitive or like it was just checking a box, I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required ones I'd force myself through even if I was bored, but optional ones had to actually grab my attention. If an optional task seemed tedious, I just wouldn't do it. The gamified elements helped blur that line a bit though—made optional stuff feel more worthwhile.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also used some YouTube videos and podcasts related to the course material that explained concepts in different ways. None of that would show up in the LMS at all.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened away from the LMS. I'd listen to downloaded lectures multiple times, talk through concepts with my roommate who's in a similar field, and just think through problems while walking around. The LMS would only show me downloading something once, not the hours I spent with it afterward.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture or PDF once and then use it repeatedly offline. My LMS activity probably looked minimal, but I was actually engaging with that material a lot—just not in a way the system could track. It's just more convenient to have things saved locally.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. I felt stressed and kind of overwhelmed, and my attendance dropped even lower than usual. I just couldn't bring myself to show up to things when I was already behind. The gamified tasks actually helped pull me back a bit because they felt manageable.
Q13: Definitely. When I had work shifts or other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Fatigue was a big factor too—some nights I'd log in intending to do something and just stare at the screen before giving up. Competing priorities meant the course often got pushed aside until the last minute.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd open a video and let it play while scrolling on my phone, or I'd click through discussion posts without really absorbing anything. Being logged in didn't mean I was actually present mentally. Sometimes I was just too tired to focus properly.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was procrastination, part of it was that I just didn't feel motivated until pressure hit. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, which was kind of true but also kind of an excuse. The anxiety of starting felt worse than the anxiety of the deadline.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd barely touch the LMS until something was due soon, then I'd scramble to catch up. My activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates and flatlined otherwise. It's not the healthiest approach but it's how I've always operated.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, especially when it was positive. Getting good marks on exams made me feel like my approach was working, even if my attendance was terrible. Negative feedback on assignments sometimes motivated me to try harder, but other times it just made me want to avoid that type of work.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I needed more external structure to stay on track. The gamified elements added some structure that helped, but overall I probably would have benefited from more mandatory checkpoints.
Q19: The flexibility made engagement harder for someone like me who drifts without deadlines. But the gamified tasks made it easier—they broke things into smaller chunks that felt achievable. The discussion board was also nice because it didn't require showing up at specific times. Synchronous requirements would have killed me.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring, trying to figure out what's expected, or just anxious about missing something. They could also be like me—opening things without really engaging, or downloading materials to use offline. High activity doesn't mean high productivity; sometimes it's just digital wandering.
Q21: That's basically my experience. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still got a distinction on my exam. I learned most of the material through downloaded lectures and my own methods outside the system. The LMS just doesn't capture how I actually study, so my low engagement numbers are misleading.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you were actually paying attention or just had something open in the background. They also completely miss offline study, which for me was where most of the real learning happened.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually understanding and caring about the material, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present and curious, even if that doesn't always translate to visible activity. I felt engaged when concepts clicked, not necessarily when I was clicking around the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned from listening to lectures repeatedly while doing other things, or the conversations I had with friends about course concepts. It also wouldn't capture the gamified tasks' effect on my mood—they genuinely made me happier about the course even when my overall engagement looked low. My exam score reflects understanding the data doesn't show.
